clean_text: Very heavy on the right side. It's very light on the left side. This is akin to the row being out of order to the row not being straight. The rows have to be straight and the rows have to be as close to the im as possible. Being on the right side of the imam is no more virtuous than being on the left side unless both sides are equal. If you walk into the masid and you find that if I stand on the right or if I stand on the left I'll be the same distance from the imam then you choose the right. any other instance you stand in the place in the row that places you closest to the imam. This affects everyone. This affects everyone that is in the prayer with us. In Ahmed, the prophet sallall.aii Wasam led and he was reciting and he slipped in a verse. And so after the he turned and he said because of the mistake that he made in the recitation he said I have made this mistake in recitation because there are those amongst us who joined the prayer that had not properly made their woo and so it affected the recitation of the imam. And so if you come to pray with us, make sure that you perfect the your will make it properly. Anything that any one of us does affects everyone else in the prayer. So we have an a reward to be had when we come together for salah. We can't get this reward anywhere else. It has to be together in jama. And when one person makes a mistake in the row or the row is not balanced or the woo is not made correctly or etc. It affects the reward of everyone. Every single one of us has their reward docked. We lose parts of our reward because of these seemingly simple mistakes. And so it's very important that when you come into the masid, you think don't just pray where the doors are or I know sometimes we have an anxiety that maybe I'm going to miss the even if you miss the it's never a justification to improperly make the salah. I mean, if you if we think about it, we all woke up at this hour. We made wool. We inconvenienced ourselves. We came all the way out here for what? For what reason? Reward. So then why would we shoot ourselves in the foot with such a silly thing? So we want to make sure that the row is always straight and that the row is always balanced. This was so important that the prophet sallallah.aii wasallam refused to start salah until every single person in the row was exactly where they were supposed to be. And this was so important that one of the miracles that the prophet sallallah.aii alaihi wasallam was given was that Allah gave him the ability only in salah to be able to simultaneously see the people in the rows behind him while he's in salah. So Allah gave him the ability to monitor everyone in the salah as long as he was in salah. That's how important it is that the rows be straight and that the rows be balanced. They have to be balanced. They have to be straight. These are not suggestions. None of these things are suggestions. These are obligations upon us. And our children are watching us. The prophet sallall.aii wasallam was narrated to have responded to salah after he had finished his wudoo. And that's actually the narration that the author mentions. summary: This lecture from the Muslim Community Center of Tucson covers two main topics: the proper conduct during congregational prayer (salah) and the Islamic greeting (assalamu alaikum). The first section emphasizes the importance of maintaining straight and balanced rows during prayer, explaining that the positioning of each individual affects the reward of the entire congregation. The speaker cites prophetic traditions showing that the Prophet Muhammad refused to begin prayer until rows were perfectly aligned, and that he was given the miracle of being able to see all worshippers simultaneously during salah. The second section discusses when wudu (ablution) is obligatory versus preferable, focusing on a hadith about Muajjir ibn Abu Umayyah greeting the Prophet during wudu, and how the Prophet taught the importance of remembering Allah in a state of purity by delaying his response to the greeting.
suggested_title: Congregation and Conduct: Straight Rows in Prayer and the Etiquette of Islamic Greetings
catchy_title: Why Your Position in Prayer Matters to Everyone
hadith_chapter: Chapter on Wudu and its rulings
